fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a HTML-to-Markdown converter library — basically something devs can call programmatically to transform HTML snippets into clean Markdown text. The output needs to be totally deterministic so we can store it or compare it reliably. Think of it like that sanitizer utility we built for the content pipeline last quarter, but this time it's about converting structure not stripping it.

The main things it needs to handle: headings, paragraphs, emphasis/bold, links (including ones with weird spaces in URLs), images, code blocks with language hints, ordered and unordered lists, tables (just the data rows, not header rows), blockquotes, horizontal rules, and line breaks. There should also be a way to tweak the output style — like different heading formats or rule styles — and a way to just skip certain element types entirely if the caller doesn't want them in the output.

It needs a proper CLI/stdin adapter so our test harness can pipe JSON in and get results back. The test runner script lives under rcb_tests/ and should be a single bash entry point. We want results written to per-case output files so nothing gets overwritten between runs.

The internal structure matters too — don't just throw everything in one file. Keep the conversion logic separate from the I/O stuff. We'll be extending this over time so it needs to be easy to add new element handlers without touching the core.

Let me know if anything's unclear — I'll be honest I'm fuzzy on some of the edge cases around inline code escaping and reference-style links.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: convert(html: str, options: dict | None = None) -> str

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Setext-style headings are supported via the `heading_style: 'setext'` option. For h1, the title is followed by a line of `=` characters matching the title length. For h2, it uses `-` characters. Only h1 and h2 support setext style; deeper headings fall back to ATX hash-prefix format. Default heading style is ATX (hash-prefix).

By default, horizontal rules (`<hr>`) are rendered as `* * *`. When the `horizontal_rule: 'dashes'` option is set, they render as `- - -`. The rule is surrounded by blank lines on both sides in block context.

By default, `
` elements render as two trailing spaces followed by a newline (` \n`). When `line_break: 'backslash'` is set, they render as a backslash followed by a newline (`\\\n`). Consecutive `
` elements each produce their own break marker.

Unordered list items default to 3 spaces of padding after the `*` marker (i.e., `*   Item`). This is configurable via `unordered_list_spacing` option (integer). For example, `unordered_list_spacing: 2` produces `*  Item`. Ordered list items default to 2 spaces after the period (i.e., `1.  Item`).

If a link's `href` or an image's `src` contains one or more space characters, the destination must be wrapped in angle brackets in the Markdown output. For example, `<a href="/Some Page.html">` becomes `[text](<https://example.com/Some Page.html>)`. No percent-encoding is applied — the raw URL is preserved, just wrapped.

When inline `<code>` content itself contains backtick characters, the wrapping backtick delimiters must expand to avoid ambiguity. Specifically, if the content contains a single backtick, use double backticks as delimiters (`` `` `code` `` ``). The number of delimiter backticks must exceed the longest run of backticks found inside the content.

trigger_keywords: only data rows in table output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Table rendering only emits `<td>`-based data rows. Rows containing only `<th>` header cells are intentionally skipped and produce no Markdown output. Each data row is rendered as a pipe-delimited line starting with `| `. An empty table (no data rows) produces an empty string.

The `omit_elements` option accepts a list of lowercase HTML element tag names (e.g., `["img", "table"]`). Any element whose tag name matches an entry in this list — case-insensitively — produces no Markdown output at all. The rest of the document converts normally. This suppression is applied at the element level, not the text level.

Language labels on fenced code blocks are detected from the `class` attribute of the `<code>` element using the `language-` prefix convention (e.g., `class="language-rust"` → ` rust`). If no `language-` class is present, the opening fence has no label (` `). The fence delimiter is triple backticks by default.

The test runner writes per-case output files to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_index>.txt`. For example, the first case (index 0) in `feature1_block_structure.json` run with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` is written to `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_block_structure@000.txt`. Different `--cases-dir` values produce separate namespaced subdirectories so runs never overwrite each other.

The final Markdown output must have all leading and trailing whitespace (including newlines) stripped from the overall result string. Internal blank lines between block elements (e.g., between headings) are preserved. This normalization applies to the final assembled output, not to individual element conversions.

The text content of an `` element is trimmed of leading and trailing whitespace before being placed inside the square brackets of the Markdown link. For example, ` bla ` becomes `[bla](/)`. This trimming applies only within the link text boundaries and does not affect surrounding document text.

trigger_keywords: separation of IO from core logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must use a multi-file layout separating concerns: core conversion logic (HTML parsing, element handlers, options) must be physically separated from the I/O adapter (stdin JSON reading, stdout writing). A recommended layout includes a `src/` directory for the library and a separate adapter/CLI entry point. No single 'god file' is acceptable given the domain complexity.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: like that sanitizer utility we built for the content pipeline last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Refers to the architectural pattern of a clean library core decoupled from I/O, analogous to the Separation of Concerns (SRP/DIP) constraints described in the PRD's Architecture & Engineering Constraints section — specifically that the JSON stdin/stdout adapter must be logically and physically separate from the converter domain logic.

context_pointers.vague_reference: I'll be honest I'm fuzzy on some of the edge cases around inline code escaping and reference-style links
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Refers to two specific hidden behaviors: (1) backtick fence expansion for inline `<code>` elements whose content contains backtick characters (C006), and (2) reference-style link output where `options.reference_links` triggers generation of numbered reference labels with definitions appended after a blank line — described in Feature 3 of the PRD but not surfaced in the fuzzy ticket.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but it falls outside the scope of what I can clarify directly. I'd recommend checking the test case JSON files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the specific feature you're asking about — each file has concrete input/output examples that serve as the ground truth contract. If the behavior you need isn't covered there, flag it and we can schedule a quick sync.

feature1_block_structure.json.description: Converts block-level HTML text structure into Markdown block syntax while normalizing surrounding whitespace.

cases.expected_output:
# Heading 1

## Heading 2

### Heading 3

#### Heading 4

##### Heading 5

###### Heading 6

feature2_inline_formatting.json.description: Converts inline emphasis and inline code elements into Markdown inline syntax without adding artificial block breaks.

feature3_links.json.description: Converts anchor elements into Markdown links, including title attributes, whitespace trimming around link text, URL escaping, and reference-style output when requested.

feature4_images.json.description: Converts image elements into Markdown image syntax using source, alternate text, and title attributes, including URL escaping for spaces.

feature5_code_blocks.json.description: Converts preformatted code blocks into fenced Markdown code blocks and includes a language label when the HTML provides one.

feature6_lists.json.description: Converts ordered and unordered lists into Markdown list rows, respecting configured marker and spacing where provided.

feature7_tables.json.description: Converts table captions and table body rows into a plain Markdown table-like row stream while empty tables produce no output.

feature8_output_style_options.json.description: Applies formatting options that change Markdown style while preserving the converted content semantics.

feature9_element_omission.json.description: Allows callers to request that selected element names be omitted from the rendered Markdown output.
